proposedFix: Proposal (human approval required): 1) Roll back the v3.2.0 http-client connection-pool configuration change to the previous known-good values OR temporarily increase the client connection pool size and reduce connection_acquire timeout to prevent long queue waits while investigating. 2) Re-deploy a configuration that restores pool.max to previous value (or to a safe higher value if rollback is not possible) and lower connection_acquire_wait thresholds so requests fail fast instead of piling up. 3) Add/verify client-side instrumentation and alerts: track pool.max, active_connections, connection_acquire_wait_ms_p95, and socket timeout counts; create an alert when active_connections == max and acquire_wait_p95 > threshold. 4) Perform a controlled canary of the config change with load to validate behavior before wide rollout. 5) Post-incident: update deployment checklist to include runbook RB-POOL-001 verification steps and schedule a follow-up to review whether default pool sizing and timeouts need revision.
postmortem: Summary: After the v3.2.0 deploy that changed http-client connection-pool tuning (E1), the payment-service hit client-side connection-pool exhaustion (active connections reached 5/5 and connection_acquire_wait_ms_p95 rose to ~9,800–15,200 ms; E6), producing many local read timeouts (E2, E3) and a spike in error rate (E5) while the bank-gateway health-checks remained healthy and low-latency (E4). Root cause: client-side connection-pool exhaustion caused by the recent configuration change. Supporting sources: runbook-connection-pool-exhaustion.md (C1) describes this failure mode and diagnostics; postmortem-2025-09-payment-pool.md (C2) documents a prior identical incident and mitigation. Actions: rolled-back or adjusted pool config (proposed), improved alerts and instrumentation, and will add canary verification to deployment checklist (proposal requires human approval).

evidence.statement: 2026-06-08T09:04:58.110Z deploy v3.2.0 was applied with an 'http-client configuration update (connection pool tuning)'.

At 2026-06-08T09:05:36.511Z the service logged 'Payment failed orderId=O-55801' with a java.net.SocketTimeoutException: Read timed out from com.shop.payment.BankGatewayClient.charge at BankGatewayClient.java:31.
Repeated 'Payment failed' errors with java.net.SocketTimeoutException: Read timed out occurred at 2026-06-08T09:05:56.834Z (orderId=O-55819) and 2026-06-08T09:06:31.118Z (orderId=O-55842).
Health-check logs show bank-gateway responded 200 OK in 118 ms at 09:05:40.118Z, in 121 ms at 09:06:10.450Z, and in 115 ms at 09:07:02.300Z.
error_rate_percent rose from 0.2% at 09:03 to 6.4% at 09:05 and to 21.3% at 09:07 within the 09:00–09:20 window.

Connection pool metrics show active connections reached the max 5/5 at 09:05 and remained at 5/5 through 09:15 while connection_acquire_wait_ms_p95 increased from 6 ms at 09:03 to 9,800 ms at 09:05 and to 15,200 ms at 09:10.

hypotheses.statement: Client-side connection-pool exhaustion introduced by the v3.2.0 http-client configuration change caused requests to queue waiting for a connection and time out locally, while the bank-gateway itself remained healthy.

Intermittent increased latency (or sporadic slow tails) in the bank-gateway for real payment requests (not surfaced by lightweight health-checks) caused connections to be held longer, driving the client pool to saturate and producing read timeouts in Payment-service.

reasons: E1 shows a deploy at 2026-06-08T09:04:58.110Z that changed http-client connection pool config, correlating temporally with the spike that follows.

E6 shows connection_pool active reached the configured max 5/5 at 09:05 and stayed there while connection_acquire_wait_ms_p95 jumped from single digits to 9,800 ms then 15,200 ms, directly supporting client-side queueing on the pool.
E2 and E3 show SocketTimeoutException read timeouts beginning at 09:05:36 and continuing, consistent with requests timing out while waiting for a connection rather than downstream failures.
E4 shows bank-gateway health-checks returned 200 OK with low latency (~115–121 ms) during the incident window, indicating the downstream remained healthy and contradicting a downstream outage.
E5 shows error_rate_percent rose sharply in the same window, consistent with client-side timeouts caused by pool exhaustion.

C1 (runbook RB-POOL-001) describes exactly this failure mode (client-side pool exhaustion after a config change) and recommends the exact diagnostics observed (check downstream health, check your own pool metrics, correlate with deploy).

C2 (postmortem PM-2025-09-PAYMENT) documents a prior similar incident where a client pool tuning caused identical symptoms and misattribution to the downstream, strengthening the plausibility of this root cause.
E4 shows bank-gateway health-checks returned 200 OK with low latencies (~115–121 ms) at 09:05–09:07, which contradicts the claim of intermittent increased latency on the downstream.

C1 explicitly states: if downstream p95 is normal and health-checks return 200 quickly, the downstream is NOT the problem — this runbook guidance contradicts the hypothesis that unseen downstream slow tails caused the pool saturation.

No evidence item (E1–E6) shows increased bank-gateway latency for real payment requests; E2 and E3 show client-side SocketTimeoutExceptions but do not provide evidence that the bank-gateway was slow or that requests reached it, so the hypothesis lacks supporting evidence from the provided items.

E6 demonstrates client-side pool saturation and large connection_acquire_wait_ms_p95, which is an alternative explanation that H2 does not address; the hypothesis fails to explain why health-checks remained fast (E4) while claiming real-payment latencies increased.

decision.rationale: Per priority rules: the Critic marked H1 as SUPPORTED and H2 as REJECTED. H1 explains the full set of observations (E1 deploy changed http-client pool; E6 pool at 5/5 and connection_acquire_wait_ms_p95 jumping to ~9,800–15,200 ms; E2/E3 repeated java.net.SocketTimeoutException read timeouts; E4 shows bank-gateway health-checks healthy and low latency; E5 error rate spike). Citations C1 and C2 explicitly describe this failure mode and a prior identical incident, supporting the mechanism. Therefore H1 best satisfies Critic support, evidence coverage, and citation backing.
decision.proposedFix: Proposal (human approval required): 1) Roll back the v3.2.0 http-client connection-pool configuration change to the previous known-good values OR temporarily increase the client connection pool size and reduce connection_acquire timeout to prevent long queue waits while investigating. 2) Re-deploy a configuration that restores pool.max to previous value (or to a safe higher value if rollback is not possible) and lower connection_acquire_wait thresholds so requests fail fast instead of piling up. 3) Add/verify client-side instrumentation and alerts: track pool.max, active_connections, connection_acquire_wait_ms_p95, and socket timeout counts; create an alert when active_connections == max and acquire_wait_p95 > threshold. 4) Perform a controlled canary of the config change with load to validate behavior before wide rollout. 5) Post-incident: update deployment checklist to include runbook RB-POOL-001 verification steps and schedule a follow-up to review whether default pool sizing and timeouts need revision.

Root cause: client-side connection-pool exhaustion caused by the recent configuration change. Supporting sources: runbook-connection-pool-exhaustion.md (C1) describes this failure mode and diagnostics; postmortem-2025-09-payment-pool.md (C2) documents a prior identical incident and mitigation. Actions: rolled-back or adjusted pool config (proposed), improved alerts and instrumentation, and will add canary verification to deployment checklist (proposal requires human approval).
